# SETUP (MCP & Skills \- Enterprise)

**Hack Day \- SETUP (MCP & Skills \- Enterprise)**

![][image1]

Today we’re launching our first Hack day with
[FiftyOne Skills](https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-skills), packaged
workflows that teach AI assistants to run computer vision pipelines. Combined
with the
[**FiftyOne MCP Server**](https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-mcp-server),
Claude becomes a data curator that actually executes, not just generates code.

Hello world: Develop plugins Skills:
[here](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/WmGGgny9Vsk)

### **What Are Skills and MCP?**

Skills and MCP solve different parts of the same problem. MCP is about
**connection**: it lets an agent talk to real systems, run real operations, and
get real results instead of just generating text. Skills are about
**guidance**: they teach the agent how to use those capabilities correctly for
a specific task. MCP exposes what the system can do, while skills explain how
and when to do it. On their own, tools are powerful but ambiguous. _Skills turn
those tools into repeatable workflows. They encode experience, decisions, and
guardrails_. Together, they let agents move from “I can call functions” to “I
know how to complete this task end to end.”

**Two Parts, One System**

**FiftyOne MCP Server** connects Agents to FiftyOne’s 80+ operators, dataset
management, model inference, brain computations, and the App. It’s the bridge
between natural language and FiftyOne tools.

**FiftyOne Skills** are expert workflows built on top. Each skill teaches the
agent how to complete a specific task: import data, find duplicates, visualize
embeddings. Skills handle the nuances so you don’t have to.

## Setup

Choose your path based on whether you are using **FiftyOne open source** or
**FiftyOne Teams (Enterprise)**.

### Open Source Setup

#### Step 1: Set Up a Python Environment

We recommend Python 3.11 in a dedicated virtual environment. If Python is not
installed on your machine, download it from
[https://www.python.org/downloads/](https://www.python.org/downloads/).

```shell
python3.11 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel
```

On Windows, activate with `.venv\Scripts\activate`.

#### Step 2: Install FiftyOne

```shell
pip install fiftyone
```

Verify:

```shell
python -c "import fiftyone as fo; print(fo.__version__)"
```

#### Step 3: Install the FiftyOne MCP Server {#step-3:-install-the-fiftyone-mcp-server}

**Note:** The MCP server is **not required** to use the
`fiftyone-develop-plugin` skill. You can build plugins without it. The MCP
server is only needed for skills that interact with live datasets (import,
export, inference, duplicates, etc.).

**Note:** Some UI controls and MCP functions that require context from the
running App (such as reading the current view, selected samples, or active
panel state) are not available when using a standard dev agent. These will only
work with the **Voxel Agent**.

```shell
pip install fiftyone-mcp-server
```

Verify by running:

```shell
fiftyone-mcp --version
```

**Note:** This command starts the MCP server, it will not return a version
number and it will not stop on its own. That is expected. You should see output
like this:

```
Starting fiftyone-mcp server...
fiftyone-mcp server initialized successfully
```

If you see these two lines, the installation is working. Press `Ctrl+C` to stop
it, your AI tool manages the server lifecycle automatically once configured.

#### Step 4: Install FiftyOne Skills

**Option A: Universal installer (recommended):**

```shell
curl -sL skil.sh | sh -s -- voxel51/fiftyone-skills
```

The installer walks you through four prompts:

1. **Which skills to install**: type `a` to install all skills at once. If you
   only want the develop-plugin skill, select option `7` instead.
2. **Which agent/provider**: select the one you are using (Claude Code, Cursor,
   VS Code, Gemini CLI, Antigravity, etc.).
3. **Install scope**: choose **global** rather than per-project. Global avoids
   having to reinstall every time you start a new project and prevents
   conflicts between workspaces.
4. **Confirmation**: review the summary and confirm to apply.

**Option B: Check the list of providers and how to install it**
[Setup Agentic Coding at Voxel51](https://docs.google.com/document/d/11k4NeeBSim5guH-6TvSk9clf-Pp3Rp0JAreA6Ztuqzg/edit?pli=1&tab=t.0#heading=h.658dliyhkimv)

#### Verify Your Setup

Open your AI agent and ask:

```
Can you see the FiftyOne skills? Can you connect to the FiftyOne MCP server?
```

The agent should confirm it has access to both. If it can see the skills and
the MCP server responds, you are ready to start building.

### Enterprise Setup (FiftyOne Teams)

#### Step 1: Create a Conda Environment

```shell
conda create --name teams python=3.11
conda activate teams
```

#### Step 2: Install FiftyOne Teams

**Option A — Install from the ephemeral dev index** (get the exact command from
your API keys settings page):

```shell
pip install --index-url "https://$(gcloud secrets versions access latest \
  --secret pypiproxy-admin-token \
  --project computer-vision-team)@pypi.dev.fiftyone.ai/simple/" \
  fiftyone==<version>
```

**Option B — Install from a local branch** (must be the `voxel-agent` branch):

```shell
pip install -e /path/to/fiftyone-teams
```

#### Step 3: Set Up Media Cache

Download `vision-storage-master.json` from GCS, then create:

```shell
mkdir -p ~/.fiftyone
```

`~/.fiftyone/media_cache_config.json`:

```json
{
    "google_application_credentials": "/path/to/vision-storage-master.json"
}
```

#### Step 4: Generate an API Key

Go to your ephemeral settings page and create a new API key.

#### Step 5: Export Credentials

```shell
export FIFTYONE_API_URI=https://<your-username>-api.ephem.fiftyone.ai
export FIFTYONE_API_KEY=<your-api-key>
```

(Optional) Add these to `~/.zshrc` or `~/.bashrc` to avoid re-exporting every
session

Make sure the `teams` conda environment is active when you run the installer.  
From here you can follow [step 3](#step-3:-install-the-fiftyone-mcp-server) in
the open source install process.

## Install Example Plugins (Optional)

If the agent has trouble generating the plugin, this repo has examples you can
clone locally and ask the agent to learn from. Great list of plugins the agent
can pull patterns from\!

```shell
git clone https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-plugins.git

fiftyone plugins download https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-plugins \
    --plugin-names @voxel51/io
```

####

## Try It: Develop a Plugin

With setup done, ask your AI tool to build a plugin using the
`fiftyone-develop-plugin` skill. For a full list of plugin ideas to try, see
the idea board:

[**Plugin Ideas Board**](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1ZypGAj33gzKL5U6w4FQhaotXW0KaF7EtqM-1WYPqC9I/edit?gid=0#gid=0)

## Launch FiftyOne

### Start the App in Debug Mode

Debug mode prints all Python and server logs to your terminal, essential when
developing plugins:

```shell
fiftyone app debug
```

With a specific dataset:

```shell
fiftyone app debug <dataset-name>
```

The App opens at `http://localhost:5151`.

Enterprise users: make sure your credentials are exported and your `teams`
conda environment is active before running this command.

### Open a Panel

_![][image2]_

1. Launch the App and load a dataset
2. Click the **\+** button in the top-right panel area
3. Select your panel from the dropdown list

### Find and Run an Operator

_![][image3]_

1. Press the **backtick key** (`` ` ``) or click the operator icon in the
   toolbar
2. Search for your operator by name (e.g. `show_heatmap`)
3. Click the operator to open its input form
4. Fill in the parameters and click **Execute**

## Connect to Your AI Tool

### Claude Code

Claude Code is the recommended provider, it has native support for both MCP
servers and the plugin marketplace.

**Install skills** inside a Claude Code session:

```shell
/plugin marketplace add voxel51/fiftyone-skills
/plugin install fiftyone-develop-plugin@fiftyone-skills
```

**Connect the MCP server** inside a Claude Code session:

```shell
claude mcp add fiftyone -- fiftyone-mcp
```

**Verify:**

```
List my FiftyOne datasets
```

### VS Code (GitHub Copilot)

**Install skills** using the universal installer from your terminal:

```shell
curl -sL skil.sh | sh -s -- voxel51/fiftyone-skills
```

Select **VS Code** when prompted.

**Connect the MCP server** by creating or editing `.vscode/mcp.json` in your
project root:

```json
{
    "servers": {
        "fiftyone": {
            "command": "fiftyone-mcp",
            "type": "stdio"
        }
    }
}
```

If `fiftyone-mcp` is inside a virtual environment and not on your PATH:

```json
{
    "servers": {
        "fiftyone": {
            "command": "/path/to/.venv/bin/fiftyone-mcp",
            "type": "stdio"
        }
    }
}
```

Install via one-click:
[Install FiftyOne MCP in VS Code](https://insiders.vscode.dev/redirect/mcp/install?name=fiftyone&config=%7B%22command%22%3A%22fiftyone-mcp%22%7D)

**Verify** in the GitHub Copilot chat panel:

```
List my FiftyOne datasets
```

### Cursor

**Install skills** using the universal installer:

```shell
curl -sL skil.sh | sh -s -- voxel51/fiftyone-skills
```

Select **Cursor** when prompted. Or use the one-click install:
[Install FiftyOne MCP in Cursor](cursor://anysphere.cursor-deeplink/mcp/install?name=fiftyone&config=eyJjb21tYW5kIjoiZmlmdHlvbmUtbWNwIn0)

**Connect the MCP server** by adding to `~/.cursor/mcp.json` (global) or
`.cursor/mcp.json` (project-scoped):

```json
{
    "mcpServers": {
        "fiftyone": {
            "command": "fiftyone-mcp"
        }
    }
}
```

If `fiftyone-mcp` is inside a virtual environment:

```json
{
    "mcpServers": {
        "fiftyone": {
            "command": "/path/to/.venv/bin/fiftyone-mcp"
        }
    }
}
```

Restart Cursor after saving the file.

**Verify** in a Cursor chat session:

```
List my FiftyOne datasets
```

### Gemini CLI

**Install skills:**

```shell
gemini extensions install https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-skills.git --consent
```

This installs all FiftyOne skills as a Gemini extension using the `AGENTS.md`
context file. The MCP server is registered automatically from
`gemini-extension.json` in the skills repo.

If you need to point to a specific environment, edit the extension config to
use the full path to `fiftyone-mcp`.

**Verify** in a Gemini CLI session:

```
List my FiftyOne datasets
```

### Antigravity

**Install skills** using the universal installer:

```shell
curl -sL skil.sh | sh -s -- voxel51/fiftyone-skills
```

Select **Antigravity** when prompted.

**Connect the MCP server** by creating or editing `.antigravity/mcp.json` in
your project directory:

```json
{
    "mcpServers": {
        "fiftyone": {
            "command": "fiftyone-mcp"
        }
    }
}
```

Restart Antigravity after saving the file.

**Verify:**

```
List my FiftyOne datasets
```

### Troubleshooting

**`fiftyone-mcp: command not found`**

The binary is not on your PATH. Find the full path and use it in your config:

```shell
which fiftyone-mcp
```

**MCP connects but returns no datasets**

Make sure datasets were created with `persistent=True`:

```py
import fiftyone as fo
dataset = fo.load_dataset("my-dataset")
dataset.persistent = True
```

**Wrong Python environment**

Always launch your AI tool from a terminal where your virtual environment or
conda environment is already active.

**Port already in use**

If you get a port conflict error when launching the App, it means another
FiftyOne session is already running on the default port (5151). Specify a
different port to avoid the conflict:

```shell
fiftyone app debug --port 5252
```

Or set it for the entire session:

```shell
export FIFTYONE_DEFAULT_APP_PORT=5252
fiftyone app debug
```

### **Resources**

-   [FiftyOne MCP Server](https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-mcp-server)
-   [FiftyOne Skills](https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-skills)
-   [FiftyOne Docs](https://docs.voxel51.com/)

### **Videos**

-   [Vibe Coding Computer vision Pipelines](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ycIh9xaX0Wc)
-   [Make your FO UI 10x better with Skills and Hybrid panel pattern](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/ba4GIPFzlW4)

# Best Practices: Developing Plugins with AI Agents

# Best Practices: Developing Plugins with AI Agents

A practical guide for getting the most out of the `fiftyone-develop-plugin`
skill when working with AI coding agents.

## 1\. Always Start in Plan Mode

Before writing a single line of code, enter plan mode and describe what you
want to build. Let the agent propose a file structure, operator design, and
implementation approach. Review it, push back where needed, and only approve
when you are satisfied.

Starting without a plan leads to mid-task pivots that waste context and produce
inconsistent code.

In Claude Code:

```
/plan
```

Describe the plugin goal clearly: what it does, what inputs it takes, what it
shows or returns, and which FiftyOne primitives it uses. The more specific you
are upfront, the less correction you need later.

## 2\. Think Like a PM: Define the Outcome First

Before asking the agent to build anything, answer these questions yourself:

-   What does a working plugin look like from the user's perspective?
-   What is the exact interaction? (button click, panel load, operator trigger,
    etc.)
-   What data does it need? (selected samples, dataset, a field name, a model?)
-   What is the success condition? (a new field appears, a panel renders, a
    file is written?)

Agents are better at building things you can describe precisely than things you
discover as you go. Write the acceptance criteria before you write the prompt.
Think of it as writing a ticket, not a chat message.

## 3\. Set Evaluation Criteria Upfront (Optional for technical people)

Define what "good" looks like technically before you start. For plugin
development, this includes:

-   The operator appears in the FiftyOne App operator browser
-   The plugin installs without errors (`fiftyone plugins list`)
-   Inputs validate correctly and surface meaningful errors on bad data
-   The operator executes without crashing on a real dataset
-   For panels: the component renders, the button triggers the correct action,
    and the results appear in the App
-   For delegated operators: the job is queued and completes without hanging

Having explicit criteria means you know when you are done. Without them, you
iterate forever.

## 4\. Ask Not What Claude Can Do for You

The agent is only as good as the context you give it. Your job is to set it up
to succeed:

-   **Share relevant code early.** If you already have a plugin structure,
    paste the key files at the start rather than letting the agent guess.
-   **Point to real examples.** Mention specific operators or panels from
    [fiftyone-plugins](https://github.com/voxel51/fiftyone-plugins) that are
    close to what you want.
-   **State constraints explicitly.** "This must work without MCP", "This is
    for FiftyOne Teams", "The output field name must be configurable" — say
    these things up front.
-   **Describe your dataset.** Tell the agent what media type, label fields,
    and schema your dataset has. It cannot query your data unless the MCP
    server is connected.

The agent responds to the quality of your input. Vague prompts produce vague
plugins.

## 5\. Ask the Agent to Explain What It's Doing

When the agent writes something you don't fully understand, stop and ask before
moving on. Accumulated confusion compounds quickly when context is limited.

In Claude Code, you can ask a quick clarifying question mid-task without
derailing the current work:

```
btw — what does ctx.trigger() do here and why did you choose it over ctx.ops?
```

The agent will explain inline and continue with the task. Understanding the
decisions being made also helps you give better feedback and spot errors
earlier.

## 6\. Manage Context Actively: Avoid Context Rot

Context rot is one of the most common failure modes when developing with
agents. As the conversation grows, the agent loses track of earlier decisions,
starts repeating itself, or contradicts previous output. Prevent it
proactively.

**Check your context window before it fills up:**

```
/context
```

**Create a checkpoint before compacting.** Before running `/compact`, summarize
the current state in a short message:

```
Before we compact: we have completed the operator skeleton and the fiftyone.yml.
The remaining work is: wire up the execute() method and test locally.
The open question is: which output type to use for the panel refresh.
```

**Compact when needed:**

```
/compact
```

**After compacting, restore the agent's memory** by pointing it back to the
plan:

```
We just compacted. Please re-read the plan and the current state summary before continuing.
```

**Use `/clear` to reset entirely** when a task is done or when the conversation
has drifted too far from the original goal:

```
/clear
```

Context is a finite resource. Treat it like memory on a device — manage it
deliberately rather than letting it fill up.

## 7\. Work in Small, Testable Increments

Do not ask the agent to build a complete plugin in one shot. Break the work
into steps and test each one before moving on:

1. Create the directory structure and `fiftyone.yml`: verify with
   `fiftyone plugins list`
2. Write the `resolve_input` method: verify inputs appear correctly in the App
3. Write the `execute` method: run it on a small dataset and check the output
4. Add the panel (if applicable): verify it renders and the button fires
5. Handle edge cases and errors

Each step gives you a working checkpoint. If something breaks, you know exactly
where it broke.

## 8\. Validate Code Quality with the Eval Plugin Skill

Before calling a plugin done, run the FiftyOne eval-plugin skill to check the
quality of what was built:

```
Use the fiftyone-eval-plugin skill to evaluate the plugin we just built.
```

The skill checks for:

-   Correct plugin structure and manifest format
-   Operator registration and schema correctness
-   Common anti-patterns (blocking calls in execute, missing input validation,
    etc.)
-   Agent-readiness and security considerations

Use the report to drive a final round of improvements before shipping or
sharing the plugin.

## 9\. Iterate Based on Real Feedback, Not Assumptions

Run the plugin against a real dataset early. Do not iterate on code in the
abstract: the FiftyOne App is the ground truth. Launch it in debug mode so you
see the full log output:

```shell
fiftyone app debug
```

When something does not work, paste the actual error or unexpected behavior
into the agent rather than describing it from memory. Exact stack traces and
log lines lead to faster fixes than paraphrased descriptions.

Share a practice that works for you, something others can try too.
